questions.question: Write a C function to reverse a singly linked list iteratively. Explain the algorithm with a diagram.
keyPoints: Use three pointers: prev, current, next
Initialize prev to NULL
Traverse the list, reversing each link: curr->next = prev
Update head to prev at end
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

questions.question: Explain how to find the middle element of a singly linked list in one pass using the two-pointer technique.
keyPoints: Use slow and fast pointer technique
Slow moves one step, fast moves two steps per iteration
When fast reaches end (NULL or last node), slow is at the middle
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)
For even length lists, returns the second middle element

questions.question: Write a C function to detect if a singly linked list has a cycle. Explain Floyd's Cycle Detection algorithm.
keyPoints: Floyd's Cycle Detection uses two pointers: slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps)
If they meet at some node, a cycle exists
If fast reaches NULL, there is no cycle
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)
Also known as the Tortoise and Hare algorithm

questions.question: Compare the time complexities of insert and delete operations at different positions in a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Insert at beginning: O(1) -- direct pointer update
Insert at end: O(n) without tail pointer, O(1) with tail pointer
Insert at position k: O(k) -- must traverse to position
Delete from beginning: O(1) -- direct pointer update
Delete from end: O(n) -- must find second-to-last node
Delete from position k: O(k) -- must traverse to position

questions.question: Write a C function to find the nth node from the end of a singly linked list in a single traversal.
keyPoints: Use two pointers technique
Move first pointer n nodes ahead of second pointer
Move both pointers together one step at a time
When first reaches NULL, second is at nth from end
Handle edge case where n is greater than the list length
